turns.content: Hey, I'm debugging a production issue where our Rust code crashes on shutdown. It seems like some Drop impl is running unexpectedly. How can I prevent that?
Hmm, that sounds tricky. Have you tried using `ManuallyDrop` and `mem::forget`? They let you manually control when the destructor runs.
Yeah, I thought of that but wasn't sure how to apply it in practice. Can you give an example?
Sure! For instance, if you have a C pointer wrapped inside a Rust struct and you need to pass it via FFI without Rust dropping the memory, you might use `ManuallyDrop`.
Okay, so I wrap the raw pointer in `ManuallyDrop`, then call `forget`?
Exactly! That transfers ownership to C and prevents Rust from dropping it.
But what if I wrap a struct that implements Drop? Will it still work?
Good question. Yes, you can do that too. Wrap the entire struct in `ManuallyDrop` and then call `forget`. The inner `Drop` won't run until you drop the outer wrapper.
Alright, so where exactly should I use this?
In FFI. Suppose you have a C function that takes ownership of some allocated memory. You'd wrap the pointer inside `ManuallyDrop`, call `forget`, pass it, and then in C land free the memory.
What if I'm building a custom collection like a stack or queue? Should I use ManuallyDrop there?
If you have internal pointers that should not be freed when the container is dropped, yeah. But be cautious — misuse can lead to dangling pointers.
Right, so in summary, `ManuallyDrop` lets me control when destructors run, and `mem::forget` transfers ownership to C?
Exactly! And remember, it's unsafe because you're bypassing Rust's usual safety guarantees.
Makes sense. So I should be careful with `unsafe` blocks here?
Absolutely! Wrap your calls to `ManuallyDrop` and `mem::forget` in `unsafe`. But if you're doing this right, the benefits outweigh the risks.
Okay, thanks for explaining that. I'll give it a shot on my codebase.
Good luck! If you hit any more issues, just ask. Happy debugging!
Hey, can you explain the difference between `ManuallyDrop` and `forget` in Rust?

`ManuallyDrop<T>` is a type that allows you to temporarily bypass the destructor for its inner value. This means the memory will still be deallocated when it goes out of scope, but no cleanup code inside `T` runs. On the other hand, calling `forget(x)` on a value drops it immediately without running any destructors at all.
So if I have some C++ code that uses pointers and raw memory, can Rust's FFI work with `ManuallyDrop`?

Yes! When interfacing with C++, you often need to pass or return raw pointers. Wrapping those pointers in a `ManuallyDrop<T>` lets you ensure that the memory is properly freed when no longer needed, even if Rust's type system otherwise would call the destructor multiple times.
What about when I want to build my own custom collection? Should I use `ManuallyDrop` then?

Absolutely. For example, in a custom Vec-like container, if you allocate raw memory and initialize elements manually, you don't want Rust's Drop trait to run twice — once when the buffer is freed and again for each element. Using `ManuallyDrop` inside your collection can prevent double drops.
But wait, if I call `forget` on a value, won't that cause memory leaks?

Yes, but only if you don't manage the memory elsewhere. So, it's crucial to ensure that after calling `forget`, the memory is manually freed in some other way — either through raw pointers or by delegating to an FFI function.
Okay, so I should be careful about order of operations when combining them?
Absolutely. `forget` should always come before any cleanup that relies on the memory being deallocated. Otherwise, dereferencing freed pointers causes undefined behavior.
Why does the drop checker even allow me to skip running Drop with ManuallyDrop? It feels like a bug that lets me leak resources without any warning.

That is exactly why you need unsafe blocks here. The compiler cannot verify your logic, so it trusts you explicitly to handle the safety guarantees yourself by placing ManuallyDrop inside an unsafe block. This prevents Drop from running on the wrapped value and allows you to take full control of the resource lifecycle.
I see, so I can just use mem::forget on a Box? That sounds dangerous but convenient for FFI.

You absolutely should not just use mem::forget on a Box in general code. For FFI, you need to wrap the data in ManuallyDrop and then call mem::forget on that wrapper so the pointer can cross the boundary without being dropped automatically when it goes out of scope.
Okay, I understand the FFI part. But what about my custom collection? I need to store raw pointers but never run Drop on them.

For your custom collection, you should use ManuallyDrop<T> where T is a type that holds the raw pointer or data. When you add items to your internal list, wrap them in ManuallyDrop and pass them through unsafe blocks so Drop never gets invoked on the inner data.
Wait, if I use ManuallyDrop, does that mean I lose the ability to clean up? How do I ensure cleanup happens manually then?

You must implement your own cleanup logic when you remove items from your collection. Since Drop is skipped, you cannot rely on automatic cleanup via panic handlers or destructors; instead, you explicitly call mem::forget on the ManuallyDrop wrapper only at the exact moment you are done with that resource.
So I can just forget the entire ManuallyDrop container and never worry about partial cleanup?

You cannot forget the container partially. You must manage every single item individually by calling mem::forget on each ManuallyDrop wrapper when it is removed from your collection. This ensures no resource leaks occur because you control exactly when mem::forget happens.
